text: During the surgery, the surgeon identifies that the afferent limb is in a dependent position, causing food to enter it instead of the efferent limb. A partial obstruction is noted at the outflow from the duodenal ileostomy to the efferent limb. The surgeon uses a ligature device to take down adhesions separating the afferent and efferent limbs.

The surgeon places anti-obstruction stitches to reorient the duodenal ileostomy, aiming to facilitate bile flow from the afferent limb to the efferent limb. The stitches are intended to prevent obstruction and ensure bile passes the pylorus smoothly.

The surgeon identifies a small piece of silk suture that has pulled out and proceeds to replace it with two simple interrupted silk sutures, aiming to change the orientation of the afferent limb and the anastomosis.

The surgeon creates an enteroenterostomy 15 centimeters below the duodenal ileostomy to prevent bile reflux into the stomach. An enterotomy is made on the afferent limb using electrocautery, followed by another enterotomy on the efferent limb, avoiding the anti-mesenteric border. The GIA stapling device is used to mate the two sides together and close the enterotomy defect.

The surgeon trims the small bowel while maintaining the current configuration of the enteroenterostomy, which appears wide open. Minimal blood loss is observed, and the procedure is completed quickly.
